text: Choose a shelter site with care. Look for natural protection from wind — a hillside, dense trees, or rock formation. Avoid hilltops (exposed to wind), valley floors (cold air settles), and dry riverbeds (flash flood risk). Check above for dead branches ("widow makers") that could fall. Ensure the site is near water but not in a flood zone. In cold weather, a south-facing slope receives the most sunlight in the Northern Hemisphere.
section: Shelter Building > Shelter Site Selection

text: The debris hut is one of the most effective emergency shelters. It provides excellent insulation and can be built without tools. Start with a ridgepole — a sturdy branch 9-12 feet long — propped against a tree or supported by a tripod at one end. The ridgepole should be just long enough for you to lie beneath. Lean branches against both sides at 45-degree angles, creating a ribbed framework. Starting from the bottom up, pile leaves, pine needles, grass, or other debris at least 2-3 feet thick over the entire structure. The thicker the debris layer, the better the insulation. Stuff the interior floor with dry leaves for ground insulation — cold ground draws heat from your body 25 times faster than cold air.
section: Shelter Building > Debris Hut

text: A lean-to is quick to build and provides good protection from wind and rain coming from one direction. Find or construct a horizontal support beam 7-9 feet long between two trees, approximately 3-4 feet off the ground. Lean branches against the beam at 45-60 degrees on the windward side. Layer vegetation, bark, or a tarp over the framework starting from the bottom up, like shingles. Build a reflector fire 3-4 feet in front of the opening — the lean-to reflects heat back toward you. The main disadvantage is exposure from the open side.
section: Shelter Building > Lean-To Shelter

text: In snow conditions, dig a snow cave into a firm snowdrift at least 5 feet deep. Tunnel in and up, creating a sleeping platform higher than the entrance — warm air rises and cold air sinks out the entrance. The walls should be at least 1 foot thick. Poke a ventilation hole through the roof with a stick — carbon dioxide poisoning is a real risk in sealed snow shelters. A snow cave maintains an interior temperature near 32°F (0°C) regardless of outside temperature. A single candle can raise the interior temperature by 10-15°F.
section: Shelter Building > Snow Shelters

text: If you have a tarp or poncho, shelter construction becomes much simpler. The A-frame configuration provides good rain protection: run a ridgeline between two trees at chest height, drape the tarp over it, and stake the edges to the ground. In heavy rain, ensure the tarp angle is steep enough (45+ degrees) for water to run off. Use rocks, logs, or dirt to seal the bottom edges against wind. A tarp can also be configured as a lean-to, open-front shelter with a fire reflector, or a simple ground cloth and overhead cover.
section: Shelter Building > Tarp Shelters

text: The ground steals body heat faster than the air. Always prioritize ground insulation over overhead cover. Use whatever is available: leaves (at least 6 inches), pine boughs (lay with stems down), grass, a foam pad, or even a pile of clothing. A bed of dry debris can mean the difference between sleeping and shivering all night. In an emergency, sit on your pack rather than the bare ground.
section: Shelter Building > Insulation Priorities

text: Water is your most critical survival need — you can survive weeks without food but only days without water. In most environments, water can be found by following animal tracks downhill, watching the flight patterns of birds (especially at dawn and dusk), looking for green vegetation in arid areas, and checking rock crevices and depressions after rain. In the morning, collect dew from grass and leaves by dragging an absorbent cloth through vegetation and wringing it into a container — you can collect over a quart per hour this way.
section: Water Procurement and Purification > Finding Water

text: In arid environments, construct a solar still by digging a hole 3 feet across and 2 feet deep. Place a collection container in the center. Cover the hole with clear plastic sheeting, sealed around the edges with dirt. Place a small rock in the center of the plastic directly over the container so the plastic forms an inverted cone. Moisture from the ground evaporates, condenses on the plastic, and drips into the container. A solar still can produce 1-3 cups of water per day depending on soil moisture and sunlight. Adding cut vegetation or non-potable water to the hole increases output.
section: Water Procurement and Purification > Solar Water Still

text: Boiling is the most reliable method to make water safe for drinking. Bring water to a rolling boil for at least 1 minute. At altitudes above 6,500 feet (2,000 meters), boil for 3 minutes because water boils at a lower temperature at altitude. Boiling kills bacteria, viruses, and parasites. If you lack a metal container, you can boil water in a wooden or bark container by placing fire-heated rocks into the water — select dry rocks from high ground to avoid steam explosions from moisture-containing rocks.
section: Water Procurement and Purification > Boiling

text: Unscented household bleach containing 5-6% sodium hypochlorite can purify water in the field. Add 6 drops (approximately 1/8 teaspoon) per gallon of clear water, or 12 drops per gallon of cloudy water. Stir and let stand for 30 minutes. The water should have a slight chlorine smell after treatment. If it does not, repeat the dose and wait another 15 minutes. Iodine tablets are another effective chemical treatment — follow package directions, typically 1-2 tablets per quart with a 30-minute wait time. Note that iodine is not effective against Cryptosporidium.
section: Water Procurement and Purification > Chemical Purification

text: A field-expedient filter can remove sediment and some pathogens. Layer materials in a container with holes in the bottom: start with a layer of gravel (2 inches), then coarse sand (2 inches), then fine sand (2 inches), then crushed charcoal from your fire (4 inches), then another layer of fine sand. Pour water through slowly and collect the filtered water below. This filter removes sediment, improves taste, and reduces some bacterial contamination, but it does not guarantee pathogen-free water. Always boil or chemically treat filtered water as well.
section: Water Procurement and Purification > Filtration

text: Store purified water in clean, covered containers. If using found containers, sanitize them first with a bleach solution (1 tablespoon per gallon, let sit 30 seconds, rinse). In a survival camp, establish a routine of purifying water in batches and maintaining a reserve. In cold weather, keep water containers close to your body or insulated to prevent freezing. A person needs a minimum of 2 quarts (about 2 liters) of water per day in moderate temperatures, and up to 1 gallon per day in hot conditions or during heavy physical activity.
section: Water Procurement and Purification > Water Storage

text: Before attempting to light a fire, prepare your materials in three categories. Tinder must catch a spark and hold a flame — dry grass, shredded bark (birch bark is excellent), dryer lint, cotton balls with petroleum jelly, fine wood shavings, or cattail fluff. Kindling consists of small twigs and sticks from pencil-lead to pencil thickness. Fuel wood ranges from finger-thickness to wrist-thickness pieces. Gather three times more material than you think you will need. All materials should be dead and dry — standing dead wood is drier than wood on the ground.
section: Fire Starting and Maintenance > Fire Lay Preparation

text: The teepee fire lay is the most versatile configuration. Place a tinder bundle on the ground. Lean small kindling sticks around the tinder in a cone shape, leaving an opening on the windward side for lighting. As the kindling catches, add progressively larger sticks, maintaining the cone shape. The teepee concentrates heat upward, making it effective for starting fires quickly. Once established, it can be converted to a log cabin or long fire configuration for sustained burning.
section: Fire Starting and Maintenance > Teepee Fire Lay

text: Stack fuel wood in alternating layers like a log cabin, with the tinder and kindling in the center. This configuration provides a stable, long-burning fire with good airflow. It is excellent for cooking because it creates a bed of coals as the structure collapses. Build it with the largest logs on the bottom and smaller pieces on top.
section: Fire Starting and Maintenance > Log Cabin Fire Lay

text: A ferrocerium rod (ferro rod) produces sparks at approximately 5,400°F (3,000°C) when scraped with a steel striker. Hold the rod steady and pull the striker toward you (rather than pushing the rod forward, which disturbs the tinder). Direct sparks into the tinder bundle. Once the tinder catches, gently blow at the base to supply oxygen. A ferro rod works when wet — scrape off the oxidized outer layer first. This is one of the most reliable fire-starting tools because it functions in rain, wind, and at any altitude.
section: Fire Starting and Maintenance > Ferro Rod and Striker

text: The bow drill is the most practical friction fire method. You need four components: a fireboard (dry softwood like cottonwood, willow, or cedar), a spindle (same wood, 12-18 inches long, thumb-thick, pointed at both ends), a bearing block (hardwood or stone with a socket), and a bow (a curved stick 2 feet long strung with cordage). Cut a notch in the fireboard to collect the hot dust. Wrap the bow string once around the spindle. Press the spindle into the fireboard socket with the bearing block, and saw the bow back and forth. The friction creates a hot coal in the notch, which you transfer to a tinder bundle and blow into flame. This method requires practice — the wood must be completely dry.
section: Fire Starting and Maintenance > Bow Drill

text: When everything is wet, look for standing dead wood and split it to access the dry interior. Birch bark contains flammable oils and burns even when wet. Feather sticks — thin curls shaved from a dry wood baton without detaching them — catch fire more easily than solid wood. Build your fire on a platform of green logs to keep it off wet ground. Create a small roof of bark or a tarp over your fire lay while establishing it. Once a fire is burning well, it will dry out and consume damp wood.
section: Fire Starting and Maintenance > Fire in Wet Conditions

text: To keep a fire going overnight, let it burn down to a bed of coals. Cover the coals with a thick layer of ash (2-3 inches). Place two or three large, green logs on top. The ash insulates the coals, and the green wood smolders slowly. In the morning, scrape away the ash, expose the coals, and add tinder and kindling to restart. This technique conserves firewood and ensures you have fire in the morning without starting from scratch.
section: Fire Starting and Maintenance > Banking a Fire for the Night

text:
The North Star (Polaris) indicates true north in the Northern Hemisphere. Locate the Big Dipper constellation — the two stars forming the outer edge of the cup point toward Polaris, which sits at the tip of the Little Dipper's handle. Polaris is the only star that remains essentially stationary while all other stars rotate around it.

During the day, the shadow-tip method works in any hemisphere. Place a straight stick vertically in the ground. Mark the tip of its shadow with a stone. Wait 15-20 minutes and mark the new shadow tip. A line drawn between the two marks runs approximately east-west, with the first mark being west. A perpendicular line from this gives you north-south.
section: Land Navigation > Finding North Without a Compass

text: In the Northern Hemisphere, point the hour hand at the sun. The midpoint between the hour hand and 12 o'clock points approximately south. In the Southern Hemisphere, point the 12 o'clock mark at the sun — the midpoint between 12 and the hour hand points approximately north. If on daylight saving time, use 1 o'clock instead of 12. This method is approximate and works best in temperate latitudes.
section: Land Navigation > Using a Watch as a Compass

text: Several natural features provide directional clues, though none is perfectly reliable alone — use multiple indicators together. In the Northern Hemisphere, moss tends to grow more heavily on the north side of trees (the shaded side), though this is inconsistent in dense forests. Spider webs are often built on the south side of trees where insects are more active in warmth. Snow melts faster on south-facing slopes. Prevailing wind direction can indicate compass direction if you know local weather patterns. Tree growth rings are often wider on the south side where more sunlight is received.
section: Land Navigation > Natural Navigation Indicators

text: Travel along linear features — ridgelines, rivers, trails, power lines, and fence lines — to maintain direction and create a mental map. Before moving, study the terrain from high ground and identify landmarks along your route. Use back-bearings: periodically turn around and memorize what the terrain looks like behind you, so you can recognize it if you need to retrace your route.
section: Land Navigation > Terrain Association

text: If lost, stop moving immediately. Use the STOP method: Sit down, Think about your situation, Observe your surroundings, Plan your next move. Continuing to walk when disoriented usually makes things worse. If you told someone your plans, stay in one location — search parties will find you faster. If you must move, travel downhill and follow water downstream — streams lead to rivers, rivers lead to settlements. Mark your route as you go with rock cairns, broken branches, or torn cloth so searchers can track you and so you don't travel in circles.
section: Land Navigation > Staying Found

text: CAUTION: Never eat any wild plant you cannot positively identify. If identification is uncertain, use the Universal Edibility Test from FM 3-05.70. Test only one plant part at a time. Fast for 8 hours before testing. Place a small piece on your inner wrist for 8 hours — if no reaction, place it on your outer lip for 15 minutes. If no burning or irritation, place it on your tongue for 15 minutes. If no reaction, chew and hold in your mouth for 15 minutes without swallowing. If no ill effects, swallow. Wait 8 hours. If no nausea, vomiting, or other ill effects, eat a small handful. Wait another 8 hours. If still no ill effects, the plant part is likely safe in moderate quantities. This process takes at least 24 hours per plant part.
section: Wild Food Procurement > Universal Edibility Test

text: Improvised fishhooks can be made from thorns, bone, safety pins, or carved hardwood. A gorge hook is a small, sharpened stick tied in the middle to the line — when swallowed by the fish, it turns sideways and catches. A simple fish trap can be built from stones in a stream: construct a V-shaped funnel with the narrow opening pointing downstream and a holding pen beyond. Fish swim in but cannot easily find the narrow exit. Hand fishing (noodling) is possible in warm, slow-moving water by reaching into undercuts and submerged holes where catfish shelter.
section: Wild Food Procurement > Fishing Without Equipment

text: Insects are an excellent emergency protein source — pound for pound, many contain more protein than beef. Grasshoppers, crickets, and grubs are widely available and safe to eat when cooked. Remove legs and wings from grasshoppers before eating (the barbed legs can catch in your throat). Cook all insects by roasting near a fire to kill parasites. Avoid brightly colored insects (warning coloration indicates toxicity), hairy caterpillars, insects with a strong smell, and any insect found on or near carrion.
section: Wild Food Procurement > Insect Gathering

text: Trapping is more energy-efficient than active hunting. Set multiple traps — even experienced trappers have a low success rate per individual trap. Place traps along animal trails, which are visible as worn paths through vegetation, compressed grass, or tracks in mud. The figure-4 deadfall is one of the simplest effective traps: three sticks notched to form a trigger mechanism that releases a heavy flat rock or log onto the animal when the bait stick is disturbed. Use local materials so the trap blends with the surroundings — animals avoid objects that look out of place.
section: Wild Food Procurement > Trapping Principles

text: In a survival situation, you burn 2,000-4,000 calories per day depending on activity level, weather, and stress. Procuring food costs energy. Focus food-gathering efforts on the highest calorie-to-effort ratio: fishing in productive waters, gathering nuts and berries in season, setting passive traps. Active hunting burns far more calories than it typically returns unless you are skilled. In short-term survival situations (under a week), prioritize water, shelter, and signaling over food — your body has sufficient energy reserves, but dehydration and exposure kill quickly.
section: Wild Food Procurement > Calories and Decision Making

text: Create signals visible from the air. The international distress signal is three of anything — three fires in a triangle, three rock piles, three gunshots. Clear an open area and create a large X or SOS using logs, rocks, or trampled snow. Letters should be at least 10 feet tall to be visible from aircraft. Use color contrast — dark materials on snow, light materials on dark ground. Signal panels or bright clothing spread on the ground are effective. At night, three fires in a triangle are the recognized distress signal.
section: Signaling and Rescue > Ground-to-Air Signals

text: Prepare three signal fires in advance and keep them ready to light quickly. For daytime signaling, add green branches, fresh leaves, or damp grass to a hot fire to create thick white smoke — visible for miles against a dark background. For dark backgrounds (forest, mountains), white smoke stands out best. Against overcast skies, add rubber, oil-soaked cloth, or plastic to create black smoke. At night, the fire itself is the signal — bright, hot fires are visible for miles from the air.
section: Signaling and Rescue > Signal Fires

text: A signal mirror is one of the most effective rescue tools — the flash can be seen for 50+ miles on a clear day. Any reflective surface works: a mirror, polished metal, glass, even a credit card. To aim, hold the mirror near your face and extend your other hand toward the target (aircraft or search party). Adjust the mirror until the reflected light illuminates your extended hand, then slowly move your hand away — the light beam now points toward the target. Sweep the horizon continuously when aircraft are heard, even if not visible.
section: Signaling and Rescue > Signal Mirror

text: Three short whistle blasts is the universal distress signal. A whistle carries much farther than the human voice and requires less energy. Commercial signal whistles can be heard up to a mile away. Blow three blasts, pause, repeat. If you hear a response (two blasts means "message received"), continue signaling to guide rescuers to your position.
section: Signaling and Rescue > Whistle Signals

text: Stay near your last known position or your vehicle/camp — searchers will start looking there. Create a large, visible camp with cleared ground, flagging material, and maintained signal fires. If you must travel, leave clear signs of your direction — rock arrows, broken branches, notes in weatherproof containers. Wear bright colors. At night, use a flashlight or headlamp to signal — even a cell phone screen is visible from a distance. If you hear aircraft, move to the most open ground available and signal immediately.
section: Signaling and Rescue > Making Yourself Findable
